food_subgroup: Braised beef sirloin with soy-calamansi sauce, served with steamed white rice and caramelized onions

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Bistek Tagalog specifically. Closest parent concept: beef dish (FOODON:03315595). No exact match available as of 2026-04-16.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bistek Tagalog — Filipino braised beef sirloin in soy-calamansi sauce with steamed white rice and caramelized onion rings; closest FoodEx2 parent: meat dish, braised beef
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Bistek Tagalog. Closest parent category: braised/stewed beef meat dish. Code not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Bistek Tagalog or equivalent Filipino braised beef dish as of 2026-04-16.

serving.description: 1 plate (~300g: 150g thinly sliced beef sirloin + 50g caramelized onion rings + 100g steamed white rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Bistek Tagalog consists of approximately 150g thinly sliced braised beef sirloin, 50g caramelized onion rings, and 100g steamed white rice, totalling approximately 300g and providing approximately 495 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table (Philippines) cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy for beef sirloin and rice components
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table; USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Bistek Tagalog is a composite dish. Per-100g values are derived by weighted component analysis: 150g beef sirloin (braised, soy-calamansi marinade) + 50g caramelized onion + 100g steamed white rice per 300g plate. FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines table used as primary source for Filipino dish composition; USDA SR Legacy used for beef sirloin cross-check. Sodium elevated due to soy sauce marinade (~380mg/100g composite). Values represent the full plate composite at 300g serving.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white rice GI moderated by protein and fat co-ingestion; analogous estimate for composite dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: White rice base GI ~73 (University of Sydney GI Database). Effective GI for the composite plate estimated at ~52 based on well-established protein and fat co-ingestion attenuation of postprandial glucose response (beef sirloin ~36g protein per plate) and calamansi organic acid buffering effect. GL per 300g plate = GI × available carbohydrate (g) / 100 = 52 × 45 / 100 = 23.4. This is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Bistek Tagalog exists in the University of Sydney database.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~1,140mg per 300g plate) from soy sauce marinade
hypertension.guidance: Bistek Tagalog provides approximately 1,140mg sodium per standard plate (380mg/100g), representing ~50% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg; patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce preparation or limit portion to half a plate, and the quercetin from caramelized onions (~8mg per serving) provides only modest antihypertensive benefit that does not offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~23.4 per plate) driven by 100g steamed white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load of approximately 23.4 per standard 300g plate is classified as medium; the 36g protein from beef sirloin significantly attenuates postprandial glucose response compared to rice alone, and substituting brown rice or reducing rice portion to 70g would lower GL to approximately 16 (low range), making this dish more suitable for glycaemic management in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (~1,140mg/plate) and moderate saturated fat (~6.6g/plate) alongside beneficial lean protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the primary concern is the high sodium content from soy sauce (~1,140mg per plate) which contributes to blood pressure elevation; saturated fat is moderate at ~6.6g per plate (2.2g/100g), and the lean beef sirloin provides beneficial iron (~4.5mg/plate) and zinc (~6.6mg/plate), but sodium reduction through low-sodium soy sauce substitution is strongly recommended to bring the dish within cardiovascular-safe sodium targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Modest anti-inflammatory micronutrients from calamansi and caramelized onions
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef sirloin provides approximately 0.1g omega-3 per 150g serving and quercetin from caramelized onions (~8mg per plate) offers mild anti-inflammatory benefit; calamansi contributes approximately 18mg Vitamin C per serving relevant for collagen maintenance, making this dish acceptable for rheumatoid arthritis patients with no specific contraindications beyond general sodium awareness.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bistek Tagalog is a classic Filipino braised beef dish consisting of thinly sliced beef sirloin marinated and slow-braised in a mixture of soy sauce and calamansi juice (a small Philippine citrus fruit similar to a cross between lime and mandarin), then topped with softened caramelized onion rings. The name derives from the Spanish word 'bistec' (beef steak), reflecting the Spanish colonial influence on Philippine cuisine. A standard plate (~300g) comprises approximately 150g braised beef sirloin, 50g caramelized onion rings, and 100g steamed white rice, providing approximately 495 kcal, 36g protein, 18g fat, and 45g carbohydrate. The dish has a savory-tangy flavor profile with umami depth from soy sauce, brightness from calamansi acidity, and sweetness from caramelized onions. Texture is tender and juicy due to the acid-tenderizing effect of calamansi on the beef. Clinically, the primary concern is high sodium (~1,140mg per plate, ~50% of WHO daily limit) from soy sauce; glycemic load is moderate (~23.4 per plate, medium category) but significantly moderated by the high protein content. Iron content is notable at ~4.5mg per plate (~30% of daily AI). Bistek Tagalog is one of the most common Filipino ulam (main dish) served in homes, Filipino restaurants, and catering contexts in the Philippines and among Filipino diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines Food Composition Table + USDA SR Legacy cross-check.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines Food Composition Table as primary source with USDA SR Legacy cross-check for beef sirloin component. Composite dish values calculated by weighted component analysis for a well-defined standard plate (150g beef + 50g onion + 100g rice = 300g). GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database white rice value moderated by established protein/fat co-ingestion attenuation principles. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Bistek Tagalog composite dish not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines table or USDA SR Legacy at the composite dish level as of 2026-04-14. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite braised dish not reported in available approved sources. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy for beef sirloin and rice components; no added hydrogenated fats in traditional preparation. 3. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate: white rice base GI ~73 (University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by beef protein (~36g/plate) and calamansi organic acid co-ingestion; no direct GI measurement for Bistek Tagalog exists in the University of Sydney database. GL per 300g plate = 52 × 45g available carbohydrate / 100 = 23.4 (medium). 4. Sodium of ~1,140mg per 300g plate (380mg/100g) reflects standard soy sauce marinade preparation; low-sodium soy sauce substitution can reduce plate sodium to approximately 750mg. 5. This is a Filipino dish, not a Singaporean dish; G6 multilingual requirements for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. Malay alias provided as relevant for Southeast Asian context. 6. Ontology codes (FoodEx2 code, SNOMED CT) are null — no exact codes exist for Bistek Tagalog in these systems; gap notes provided. Closest parent descriptions documented to satisfy G5 ontology honesty. FoodOn URI for 'beef dish' (FOODON:03315595) has been added as the closest parent.
